title: Why do most backtests on M1 data give unrealistic results? (honest breakdown)
author: FantasticShine4012
selftext:
I've been down a rabbit hole on data quality lately and wanted to share what I found because I don't see this discussed enough.

Most M1 backtests are broken before you even write a single line of strategy logic. Here's why:

1. Broker data has survivorship bias in the spread

Backtested spread != live spread. Especially around rollover and news.

2. Missing bars get forward-filled silently

Most platforms don't warn you. Your "open" on bar N is actually the close of bar N-3.

3. Timezone misalignment

Broker exports often shift DST differently depending on server location. This alone can add or remove fake signals.

4. Volume data is often fabricated on Forex

It's tick count, not actual volume. Still useful, but treated as "volume" it misleads.

I tested this on 5 different data sources for EURUSD 2018-2023. The same strategy ranged from -12% to +34% drawdown depending purely on which dataset I used.

Anyone else done systematic comparisons? Curious what methodologies people use to validate their data before trusting a backtest.

comments.author: Fun-Society-1763
comments.body: This is exactly why so many forward tests fail. The silent forward-filling of missing bars and spread bias in M1 data is a massive trap. If you're building serious models, you need institutional-grade data that doesn't hide these gaps. Platforms like QuantPlace were built specifically to fix this. Their datasets are pre-cleaned for survivorship bias and feature continuous 1-minute OHLCV that doesn't fake missing bars. It might save you some headaches in your backtesting loop

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
interesting, hadn't heard of QuantPlace. what's 
the pricing like? i've been comparing a few 
sources lately and the quality difference is 
pretty significant even between paid options

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
checked it out, interesting platform. though it's 
a marketplace so quality depends on who uploaded 
the data. had mixed results with crowdsourced 
datasets before — anyone verified their OHLCV 
specifically?

replies.author: Fun-Society-1763
replies.body: they have their own quality evaluator, you can try it in their tools too

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
good to know, will check the evaluator. though 
i'm more interested in whether the underlying 
data is verified before it hits the marketplace. 
free samples help but if the methodology isn't 
transparent it's hard to trust for serious 
backtesting

replies.author: Fun-Society-1763
replies.body: As I now they run the evaluator on dataset posting, so without it you can not post

comments.author: Kindly_Preference_54
comments.body: This depends on the strategy. My tests on 1m OHLC give absolutely realistic results. I base 80% of my tests on them. My strategy is swing. If it were scalping, I'd have to test on ticks.

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body: Exactly this — M1 OHLC covers the vast majority of strategies. If you ever need a clean, ready-to-use M1 dataset across stocks, forex, crypto and indices without the hassle of downloading and cleaning raw data yourself, check out powakadata.com 🙂

replies.author: Kindly_Preference_54
replies.body: \[facepalm\] that's an ad of price data that I can get for free in my MT5

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
yeah MT5 data is free, but have you actually 
validated it? gaps around NFP, DST shifts, 
forward-filled missing bars. tested the same 
EA on MT5 export vs clean source and got 16% 
difference in drawdown. free doesn't mean accurate

replies.author: Kindly_Preference_54
replies.body: My live trading is identical to my backtest of the same period. Isn't that enough?

replies.author: FantasticShine4012
replies.body:
that's great, genuinely. but most people aren't 
that lucky. one strategy working doesn't mean 
the data is clean, it means your strategy 
happened to survive the noise
